issue_title: Add --map-http-status global option to convert HTTP statuses into nonzero exit codes
issue_body:
Karl suggested this a while back, and I've had it in my queue for a while.
I definitely want this before we consider the CLI done, but I'm not sure when we'll get to it.

Several commands have a set of non-200 HTTP responses that might still be considered part of normal operations. For example, a command like `globus transfer endpoint delete ...` could be considered successful on a 200 or a 404.

It may be desirable for us to hand that information -- the 404 response, vs. a 403 or other error, to the caller, but doing so with some fixed mapping is burdensome for scripting (you need to know our statuses table), and not very extensible if we omit codes that we start using later.
The generic solution is to just let the invoking script specify what it would like us to translate various HTTP statuses into.
That lets them get meaningful status codes, avoids a magical lookup table, and makes those scripts more readable because they specify what the various error codes that they're dispatching on mean / come from.

So, the basic form would be something like this:
```bash
globus transfer endpoint delete --map-http-status "404=54" ...
```

and we can even allow crazy things like this check for nonexistence:
```bash
globus transfer endpoint show --map-http-status "404=0" --map-http-status "200=1"
```

In the original version of this proposal the idea was to restrict the caller to using the range from 50 to 99 (reserving other exit codes for our own purposes into the future).
However, I think there's a case for allowing clients to say "allow 404s" by mapping to 0.
Whether or not we allow mapping to 1 is a little bit more tenuous, but I think still acceptable, since it just means "generic error".

There's also a question of whether or not it should be
```bash
globus transfer endpoint delete --map-http-status "404=54" --map-http-status "403=53"
```
or
```bash
globus transfer endpoint delete --map-http-status "404=54,403=53"
```

That second is a little bit nicer, but also a little harder to implement.

issue_title: Make `-v/--verbose` a global option
issue_body:
I'm thinking the following:

`-v` is a "counting" option. i.e. we set `verbosity=N` where `N` is the number of occurrences of the option.
Allows `-vvv`, etc. a.k.a. "angry scripting mode", since it often means "Why won't you say what's wrong?!"

We allow a config parameter, `verbosity`, which is an int. In actual fact, `verbosity` is `max(config_verbosity, command_line_verbosity)`
Default is, of course, 0.

`verbosity` is attached to the `CommandState`, for easy access.

In addition to being able to get it as an int, have a bool helper function, `is_verbose()` for commands which only have a simple notion of verbosity.

issue_title: Make shell completion supported, change recommended sourcing method
issue_body:
cc @ranantha, I'd like us to make the current completion support an officially supported part of the CLI for GlobusWorld. I think that would be a nice time to add it in; it's a bit of a showy feature which has been done for a while and therefore requires basically no additional work.


The code has been sitting almost entirely untouched (and working) for over a year.
I've been using the current shell completion code for a while now and haven't had any issues with it. It works really well, especially in ZSH where the completion support is a bit superior.


A lot of tools use a nice trick for getting their shell completion stuff into people's bashrc:
`mytool --completion` prints the completer, and users are told to `eval "$(mytool --completion)"`

I like this. Could we add it, maybe move shell completion into the packaged CLI source dir?
We could then document this as a full feature of the CLI.

One thing that we'd need to add, I think, is some way to determine which shell to use. I think `bash` is a sane default, so:
`globus --completion` can do bash completion, and
`globus --completion zsh` can do `zsh` completion


@corpulentcoffee, @jaswilli, I know you guys have been less keen on shell completion than I. Does the change I'm suggesting (putting the current completion scripts into a hidden `--completion` option and documenting it) sound acceptable?

issue_title: TypeScript: Would be useful to have named types for complex args
issue_body:
Seems like this might already be tracked somewhere, but I couldn't find an issue. Apologies if I missed something.

The TypeScript declarations are super helpful for discovering what options are available on various resources. However, there are many deeply nested configuration objects that are built almost entirely of `pulumi.Input`, anonymous objects, and primitives. If you make a small error in a nested attribute, `tsc` produces pretty large and obnoxious error messages that are difficult to read. The entire root configuration object gets a squiggly red underline!

I've found that a great way to deal with this problem is to build up larger objects incrementally and assign explicit types to the intermediate values. Although restructuring syntax helps, this is a little more verbose, but well worth it, as it significantly narrows the scope of compiler errors.

Sometimes, you can use fancy TypeScript features like indexed types to get at a useful anonymous type for this purpose. For example:

```typescript
let defaultCacheBehavior: aws.cloudfront.DistributionArgs['defaultCacheBehavior'] = { ...
```

But that's pretty kludgy and sometimes not possible, for example when something is wrapped in `pulumi.Input<...>[]`.

Would be nice if all of these various properties had usefully named types. For this example, I'd love to write:

```typescript
let defaultCacheBehavior: aws.cloudfront.DefaultCacheBehavior = { ...
```

Generally, there are sensible names for these things in the CloudFormation documentation. Again, for example: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-properties-cloudfront-distribution-defaultcachebehavior.html

issue_title: Use HTTP/2 streaming or "long polling" for HTTP API endpoints instead
issue_body:
Work item tracking us either supporting streaming or long polling on some of our longer-running HTTP API endpoints.

The motivating example is an update going to the Pulumi Cloud. We poll looking for new updates from the PPC, when a streaming API might be more appropriate. (Although it might make other cases, like using the API via `cURL` harder.)

Alternatively, we could just pass a `--maxDelay` flag, and have the HTTP endpoint just wait until it has more data to render (e.g. "long polling", "hanging RPC", "hanging GET", or something.)

issue_title: [Enhancement] PEP 0466 - SSL - Changes consideration
issue_body:
As of Python 2.7.9 the default SSL behavior has changed. SSL certificates are verified in *strict* mode. Since vCenter's default *beginner* use case is to deploy with a self-signed certificate we may need to add some additional flags, settings, and warnings inside `pyVmomi` to alert about and accommodate this change.

* We want new users to be able to *just get going*
* We don't want to encourage *weak* SSL which is a security concern
* We do want to inform a beginner user of what's going on and what to do about it
* We want this to be as easy as possible without sacrificing security

NOTE: *this may have already been covered, this issue is here as a note to force me to examine the issue again just before the next roll out of pyVmomi*

issue_title: Provide IPython script magics
issue_body:
I find the `%%shell` magic frustrating because of its persistent connection - my input is frequently out of step with output captured form the persistent shell process. I also missed the various script cell magics like `%%bash` which are provided by the core ipython implementation at https://github.com/ipython/ipython/blob/6bf8bd1f5cf6a35a805b74985b3cce6559d59b26/IPython/core/magics/script.py. It seems that magic handling is significantly different in IPython core vs metakernel, so I had to create a wrapper class to adapt the core-provided class.

I've made an implementation for my own use, and wondered whether it's something you might consider adding to metakernel - see my gist at https://gist.github.com/jcb91/28999015f787ddffa8d3c11ecda8ebe8.

Any thoughts?

issue_title: `python setup.py test` does not install packages in `tests_require`
issue_body:
I found this issue in CCDproc, but I report it here because it seems to be an issue of astropy-helpers.

ccdproc requires the module pytest-capturelog and specifies that in its `tests_require` in setup.py.
( https://github.com/astropy/ccdproc/blob/master/setup.py#L98 )
I don't have pytest-capturelog installed on my machine and the corresponding tests fail with
```
        fixture 'caplog' not found                                               
        available fixtures: tmpdir_factory, pytestconfig, ccd_data, cache, recwarn, monkeypatch, triage_setup, record_xml_property, capfd, capsys, tmpdir         
        use 'py.test --fixtures [testpath]' for help on them.                    
```

Theses failures go away after manually installing pytest-capturelog.
Thus, it seems that either `python setup.py test` does not install the required dependencies or it installs them in a location where they are not found.
(My understanding is that `tests_require` dependencies are installed in a temporary directory only, but I might be wrong.)

There are two obvious workarounds: Installs test dependencies by hand or put them into `install_requires` even though they are only needed for testing.
(Note that this does not show up as an issue in the travis, because this dependency is installed by hand here https://github.com/astropy/ccdproc/blob/master/.travis.yml#L101 )

cc: @mwcraig

issue_title: Check unchecked types in certbot-dns-route53
issue_labels: [{'id': 277080619, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20code%20health', 'name': 'area: code health', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 263357278, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20testing', 'name': 'area: testing', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 149596829, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/good%20first%20issue', 'name': 'good first issue', 'color': 'c61aff', 'default': True}, {'id': 281347824, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/has%20pr', 'name': 'has pr', 'color': '0052cc', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
This issue is for checking untyped definitions in the `certbot-dns-route53` module.

To start, add the following to `mypy.ini` in the root of the repo:
```
[mypy-certbot_dns_route53.*]
check_untyped_defs = True
```

Then run `tox -e mypy`. If passes, submit a PR. If not, fix errors and submit a PR when tests pass. You can read more about how all of this works below.

See explanation here: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1fp0tPP9bzYkUViTnOXAyoT_KfgTfs8HSpNMoU8Tm_SY/edit?usp=sharing

From http://blog.zulip.org/2016/10/13/static-types-in-python-oh-mypy/:

Add the `--check-untyped-defs` option to your mypy arguments, and get that running with no errors on the codebase. This option causes mypy to check every def in the codebase for internal consistency; mypy can detect many classes of bugs and mistakes in your codebase, without your having written a single type annotation!

In most cases, you’ll want to fix bugs and bad code as you go, but you can also use #type: ignore annotations or exclude files to defer issues. For example, we excluded all of Zulip’s tests at first, since they’re both lower value to type-check and had most of the monkey-patching and other questionable Python in the project. For Zulip, getting clean `--check-untyped-defs` output took me about 2 days of hard work, including merging fixes for about 40 issues in the Zulip codebase.

I spent another day or two generating nice reproducers for the mypy bugs I’d encountered and improving typeshed. Now that mypy is no longer in its infancy, mypy bugs are increasingly rare. But a large project should expect to encounter and fix typeshed issues (just submit a PR!).

Once `mypy --check-untyped-defs` passes on your codebase, you should lock down your progress by running the mypy checker in your CI environment.

Because mypy type annotations are optional, once you’ve done the setup above, you can start annotating your codebase at whatever pace you like. Over time, you’ll get the benefits of static types in the parts of the codebase that you’ve annotated, without needing to do anything special to the rest of the codebase (the wonders of gradual typing!). In the next section, I’ll discuss strategy for how to move the codebase towards being fully annotated.

issue_title: Adding an option to set index.merge.scheduler.max_thread_count
issue_body:
A common case for Curator is to move indices from Hot nodes to Warm nodes. A common scenario is that the Hot nodes are backed by SSD disks and the Warm nodes are backed by spinning disks. For spinning disks, it is recommended to have the option `index.merge.scheduler.max_thread_count` set to 1.
This parameter in version 5 is an index level parameter, only dynamically settable - when trying to set it in the yml files, the following error happens: 
```
Since elasticsearch 5.x index level settings can NOT be set on the nodes
configuration like the elasticsearch.yaml, in system properties or command line
arguments.In order to upgrade all indices the settings must be updated via the
/${index}/_settings API. Unless all settings are dynamic all indices must be closed
in order to apply the upgradeIndices created in the future should use index templates
to set default values. 
```

It would be great to be able to configure this parameter, so in the Hot(ssd)-Warm(spinning) architecture it can be tuned to have the optimal value in the warm nodes.

issue_title: Declaring traittypes in Containers as uninstantiated classes
issue_body:
Feel free to ignore this, but I wasn't around when #51 was discussed… and this just came up in jupyter/nbconvert#695. So I wanted to weigh in on the question around deprecating a particular way of using uninstantiated traitlets. There are two ways of using traitlets that were deprecated in 4.1 that were not documented in the migration docs http://traitlets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/migration.html. These use cases involve traitlets being used in slightly different ways. I think we should revisit the decision to deprecate one of them. Regardless of whether we continue to deprecate that case, the deprecations should be mentioned in the migration docs.
Click for more details about these two cases and why I think they are different.

 1. traitlets that are actually instantiated on a class. ```python class MyClass(): foo = Unicode() # ✓ foo = Unicode # deprecated ``` 2. traitlets defining which types are valid inside traitlets subclassed from `Container` (e.g., `List`). ```python class MyClass(): foo = List(Unicode()) # ✓ foo = List(Unicode) # deprecated ``` Deprecating 1. was absolutely the right call. I think that we should revisit the idea of deprecating 2..
Click here for my argument as to why we shouldn't deprecate use case 2.

In case 2, the actual use of a traitlet instance is somewhat strange from a type checking perspective. What you are effectively doing is declaring that the elements of this container need to be of the same _type_ as element that is listed. The only way I've ever seen type checking work involves declaring the _type_s that should be allowed, in python that would be the _class_es that should be allowed. To follow the requirement that we only allow traitlet instances there would be analogous to a type checking syntax that declared that something should be a string by using literal strings. For example, the analogous case from the python type [annotations PEP][pytypes] was: ```python def greeting(name: str) -> str: return 'Hello ' + name ``` but if using our convention it would be ```python def greeting(name: str()) -> str(): return 'Hello ' + name ``` or equivalently: ```python def greeting(name: '') -> '': return 'Hello ' + name ``` I realise that from an implementation point of view this introduces overhead because we also need to be able to put constraints on particular values, which means we can't only work on classes.
**tldr**:; The use case in 2. is similar to type checking, and declaring types as uninstantiated classes is a standard way of specifying types in signatures (including in [python type annotations][pytypes]).
[pytypes]: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0484/

issue_title: Deleting notebook running notebook and creation of new notebook with same name.
issue_body:
1. Create a notebook (`Untitled.ipynb`)
2. run `a = 100` in the first cell
3. delete the notebook from the file system (from a console)
4. Create a new notebook. The same `Untitled.ipynb` is created.
5. Apparently, it is connected to the same old kernel as the first version of `Untitled.ipynb`.
   (a is 100, prompt number starts a 2.)

issue_title: Add column rulers to codemirror input cells
issue_body:
There is a codemirror addon called "rulers.js" that allows you to add vertical lines into the editor. This would be useful to gauge how far you are on an 80/100/120 column limit. In fact it looks like the addon is already packaged into IPython.

```
site-packages » find ./ -name rulers.js                ~/.venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages  
./IPython/html/static/components/codemirror/addon/display/rulers.js
```

From http://codemirror.net/doc/manual.html#addons :
> [display/rulers.js](http://codemirror.net/addon/display/rulers.js)
>    Adds a rulers option, which can be used to show one or more vertical rulers > in the editor. The option, if defined, should be given an array of {column [, className, color, lineStyle, width]} objects >or numbers (wich indicate a column). The ruler will be displayed at the column indicated by the number or the column property. The className property can be used to assign a custom style to a ruler. [Demo here](http://codemirror.net/demo/rulers.html).
> 
> http://codemirror.net/demo/rulers.html

issue_title: Support for gpg2?
issue_body:
Hi!

I'm using `gpg 2.1` and I'm unable to use `sops` because it can't find the new `pubring.kbx` that `gpg2` uses. Looks like it's hardcoded to open [pubring.gpg](https://github.com/mozilla/sops/blob/0483f8a07af035f2cb75e7a72b1901eccac82ed1/pgp/keysource.go#L154).

After digging a bit more, I'm not sure `crypto/openpgp` supports `gpg > 2` :frowning: 

I'm guessing I can't use `sops` unless I have `secring.gpg` and `pubring.gpg` and not `pubring.kbx`?

issue_title: Add middleware to force OIDC in specific URLs
issue_body:
After a discussion with @glogiotatidis it looks like it might be useful to have a way to force OIDC authentication in specific site URLs. The reason behind it is that there might be projects that use multiple authentication backends but for security reasons they want to force OIDC in high risk endpoints (aka /admin).

An idea could be to ship a middleware with ``mozilla-django-oidc`` that forces OIDC authentication to a configurable list of URL regexes.

issue_title: [Discussion] Object Design
issue_body:
**Background:** Attempting to include subword tokenizers as an option. The problem I am facing is the subword tokenizer requires the ability to define its own vocabulary.

The above scenario got me going down the rabbit hole of abstractions. 

**Couple questions:**

- The Field object defines instructions for converting text to a tensor. The Batch object defines how to batch tensors. The Field object has methods converting batch text into batch tensors. Another possible abstraction is the field object is only able to deal with one example. The Batch object defines how to deal with multiple examples.
- Tensor2Tensor defines the functionality of converting text to tensors as a "TextEncoder". "TextEncoder" to me is a clearer object name than "Field". Should we rename the "Field" object?
 https://github.com/tensorflow/tensor2tensor/blob/master/tensor2tensor/data_generators/text_encoder.py
- For a subword tokenizer, it requires a subword vocabulary to be built. This indicates that the subword tokenizer should have a separate field object that overrides build_vocab. 

This would not be consistent with moses and spacy tokenizers. They do not require their own field object. 

For consistency the two options are:
- Should all tokenizers have their own field object? 
- Should all tokenizers be handled in one field object?

issue_title: get_observer_look turns xarray.DataArray objects into dask.array objects
issue_body:
#### Code Sample, a minimal, complete, and verifiable piece of code

```python

import datetime
import pyorbital.orbital
import xarray

lat = lon = xarray.DataArray([[1,2],[3,4]], dims=("x", "y"))
satlat = satlon = xarray.DataArray([[0]*2]*2, dims=("x", "y"))
times = xarray.DataArray([[datetime.datetime.now()]*2]*2, dims=("x", "y"))
satelevs = xarray.DataArray([[1000]*2]*2, dims=("x", "y"))
groundelevs = xarray.DataArray([[0]*2]*2, dims=("x", "y"))
times = xarray.DataArray([[datetime.datetime.now()]*2]*2, dims=("x", "y"))
(az, elev) = pyorbital.orbital.get_observer_look(satlon, satlat, satelevs,
                    times, lat, lon, groundelevs)
print("azimuth", az)
print("elev", elev)
```
#### Problem description

The `az` azimuth output is of type `dask.array`.  The coordinates and any other metadata stored alongside the arrays in the `DataArray` are lost.  I am not familiar with `dask.array`.

This is a consequence of the [if hasattr(az, 'chunks')](https://github.com/pytroll/pyorbital/blob/master/pyorbital/orbital.py#L114) block.  The `else:`-block should work fine for `xarray.DataArray` objects, which also happen to have this attribute.

#### Expected Output

I would expect both `az` and `elev` to be of `xarray.DataArray` type, such that no information is needlessly lost.

#### Actual Result, Traceback if applicable

```
azimuth dask.array<rad2deg, shape=(2, 2), dtype=float64, chunksize=(2, 2)>
elev <xarray.DataArray (x: 2, y: 2)>
array([[79.686688, 69.883718],
       [60.955333, 53.06458 ]])
Dimensions without coordinates: x, y
```

#### Versions of Python, package at hand and relevant dependencies

Python 3.7.0, `pyorbital 1.3.1`, from conda-forge.

issue_title: Suggestions: Warn for non-string keys or values in kwargs in <class>.from_str_params()
issue_body:
I figured that when using the class method [from_str_params()](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/luigi/task.py#L296-L310), if accidentally submitting values that are not strings, one can get hard to debug errors.

E.g. with the following snippet, where the zero in the dictionary is an int rather than a string:
```python
import luigi

class ATask(luigi.Task):
    avalue = luigi.IntParameter()
    def output(self):
        return 'afile.txt'
    def run(self):
        with self.output().open('w') as outf:
            outf.write(str(self.avalue))

if __name__ == '__main__':
    t = ATask.from_str_params({'avalue': 0})
```

... I get the following error:

```bash
$ python error.py 
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "error.py", line 12, in <module>
    t = ATask.from_str_params({'avalue': 0})
  File "/home/samuel/.pyenv/versions/2.7.8/lib/python2.7/site-packages/luigi/task.py", line 297, in from_str_params
    value = param.parse_from_input(param_name, params_str[param_name])
  File "/home/samuel/.pyenv/versions/2.7.8/lib/python2.7/site-packages/luigi/parameter.py", line 296, in parse_from_input
    (param_name, "--" + param_name.replace('_', '-')))
luigi.parameter.MissingParameterException: No value for 'avalue' (--avalue) submitted and no default value has been assigned.
```

This specific error, I figured was due to the [line "if not x" in line 291 of parameter.py](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/luigi/parameter.py#L291), which results in a "no" in this if statement, due to the integer zero value being sent.

Maybe one could add checks that all the keys and values really are string, in from_str_params()?

issue_title: Add a chat on Gitter icon?
Demos in docs are still using print XXX, how about using print(XXX) for better python3 support?

I saw that Luigi says to be supporting both python2 and python3, but in the documents of [Running from the Command Line](https://luigi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/running_luigi.html), the demos are still using print in a way like "print self.x + self.y", which python3 doesn't support.

print self.x + self.y

So I think using "print(self.x+self.y)" instead may be better, because print() function is supported by both python2 and python3.
By fixing this, the docs and demos could be more friendly to beginners :).
PS. This "print" may also exist in other documents, I think these should all be fixed.

issue_title: test_external_task_retries in worker_test fails since #1449
issue_body:
Or in other words, an error email is sent when `ExternalTask`s fail, even though no email should be sent(?).

`git bisect` says that f24579704e048b25e47d393af9ebadf843f88ddc is the first bad commit, which is #1449. I think the reason is that f24579704e048b25e47d393af9ebadf843f88ddc [adds an `expl`][1] when failing, but [worker.py still assumes that 'If no `expl`, it is because of a retry-external-task failure'][2], and uses that to check when error emails should be skipped. So since `expl` is no longer `None` there, and email will be sent, even for `ExternalTask`s.

Not entirely sure what the right solution is, but I guess some more robust way of detecting if it is an `ExternalTask` that failed? Would checking `not isinstance(task, ExternalTask)` work, or are there cases where `ExternalTask`s fail for other reasons, and then the error email _should_ be sent?

[1]: https://github.com/spotify/luigi/pull/1449/files#diff-b438c8dc80f50172445c72006dc7c8f7R168
[2]: https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/luigi/worker.py#L769

issue_title: Websocket does not register disconnected Safari browser
issue_body:
With [Websockets](https://websockets.readthedocs.io), when Safari is reloaded or tab closed, the websocket server registers a closed connection. This is not the case with Aiohttp. But Aiohttp correctly registers the disconnection with Chrome. I don't know anything about the websocket protocol internals, but I guess there is a difference in what both browsers send on tab close/reload. In any case, Websockets somehow registers that event in both browsers, but Aiohttp doesn't.

Take the following app. When going to http://localhost:8080/websockets and reloading the site several times, the app emits alternating "Got client", "Lost client" with both Safari and Chrome. Now go to http://localhost:8080/aiohttp: only Chrome triggers lost connections.

```python
import asyncio
from aiohttp import web
import websockets

site = """\
<!doctype html>
<script>
const ws = new WebSocket(`ws://{href}`);
</script>
<body></body>"""


async def websockets_handler(ws, path):
    print('Got client')
    while True:
        try:
            print(await ws.recv())
        except websockets.ConnectionClosed as e:
            break
    print('Lost client')


async def aiohttp_ws_handler(request):
    ws = web.WebSocketResponse(autoclose=False)
    await ws.prepare(request)
    print('Got client')
    async for msg in ws:
        print(msg)
    print('Lost client')
    return ws


async def site_handler(request):
    if request.path == '/aiohttp':
        href = 'localhost:8080/ws'
    elif request.path == '/websockets':
        href = 'localhost:6060'
    return web.Response(text=site.format(href=href), content_type='text/html')


asyncio.get_event_loop().create_task(websockets.serve(websockets_handler, 'localhost', 6060))

app = web.Application()
app.router.add_get('/aiohttp', site_handler)
app.router.add_get('/websockets', site_handler)
app.router.add_get('/ws', aiohttp_ws_handler)
web.run_app(app, host='localhost', port=8080)
```

issue_title: Remaining feature discrepancy between TestSuite and ItsdbProfile
issue_body:
The new `TestSuite` class in the `itsdb` module is meant to replace the `ItsdbProfile` class, but there are some remaining features that don't have an equivalent in the new class. Not all features are necessary, but some are important, and `ItsdbProfile` should not be removed until the features are covered. This issue is meant for tracking which features remain unimplemented.

- [ ] filtering on column values (see #138)
- [ ] mapping to a new relations file (e.g., `ts.write(relations='.../relations')`)
- [ ] incremental file reading/writing
  This would be particularly useful for `process()`, as the full processing results for a single table can take up gigabytes of memory

issue_title: Pred hashing not consistent with Pred equality
issue_body:
The Pred component class compares with strings that may or may not end in `_rel`:

```python
>>> p = Pred.stringpred('udef_q_rel')
>>> p == 'udef_q'
True
>>> p == 'udef_q_rel'
True
>>> p == '_udef_q'
False
>>> p == 'udef'
False
```

But this does not work with hashing (e.g. in a dict or set):

```python
>>> Pred.stringpred('udef_q') in set(['udef_q', 'proper_q'])
True
>>> Pred.stringpred('udef_q_rel') in set(['udef_q', 'proper_q'])
False
>>> Pred.stringpred('udef_q') in set(['udef_q_rel', 'proper_q'])
False
>>> Pred.stringpred('udef_q') in set([Pred.stringpred('udef_q_rel'), Pred.stringpred('proper_q')])
False
>>> 'udef_q' in set([Pred.stringpred('udef_q_rel'), Pred.stringpred('proper_q')])
False
>>> 'udef_q_rel' in set([Pred.stringpred('udef_q_rel'), Pred.stringpred('proper_q')])
True
>>> any(Pred.stringpred('udef_q_rel') == q for q in set(['udef_q', 'proper_q']))
True
```

Hashing consistently on the short form would help when a Pred object is checked against Pred objects in a hash, but not when strings are used. I can't think of a good way around the latter issue, so maybe some documentation needs to be written about that.

issue_title: API for re-sending transactions
issue_body:
### What was wrong?

There are a few legitimate use cases for wanting to re-submit a transaction that has already been sent with modified parameters.

* Resending the same transaction with a higher gas price during times of network congestion.
* Quickly overriding a transaction sent in error (also by using a higher gas price)
* In a case where you *know* there is a signed transaction that was sent but never included in a block, ensuring that it will not be applied at some point in the future.

### How can it be fixed?

Implement a new API `web3.eth.overrideTransaction(txn_hash, txn_overrides)`

- `txn_hash` is the hash of the transaction you wish to override.
- `txn_overrides` is a dictionary of transaction parameters which should be used in place of the existing transaction parameters.

Behavior is as follows.

- *if* no transaction can be found with the given hash an exception is thrown.
- *if* the transaction has already been mined an exception is thrown.
- The `txn_overrides` may contain any/all fields that are normally accepted as transaction params except `nonce`.
- If `gas_price` is included in the `txn_overrides`, it **must** be greater than the existing transaction gas price (TODO: should we allow a way to bypass this validation)
- if `gas_price` is **not** included in the `txn_overrides` we should ???? (TODO: how do we choose an appropriate gas price?).

issue_title: Implement precompiled contracts
issue_body:
The EVM specification defines four so-called ‘precompiled’ contracts. The four contracts in addresses 1, 2, 3 and 4 execute the elliptic curve public key recovery function (ecrecover), the SHA2 256-bit hash scheme, the RIPEMD 160-bit hash scheme and the identity function respectively.

Call to the ecrecover precompiled contract in assembly:

CALL 0x1

Currently, LASER-Ethereum ignores calls to the precompiled contracts. In this task, the precompiled contracts are to be implemented. When a precompiled contract is called, the result should be computed using the appropriate Python cryptographic libraries. Also create tests for each of the four precompiles.

Go-Ethereum implementation:

https://github.com/ethereum/go-ethereum/blob/master/core/vm/contracts.go

This is an ongoing TopCoder challenge:

https://www.topcoder.com/challenges/30065730

issue_title: Test suite is broken for Python 3.x
issue_body:
The module `test.test_support` exists only in Python 2. This was renamed to `test.support` in Python 3. I am shipping the following patch in the Debian package for this module:

```
Description: Use test.support if Python 3 to enable test suite on Python 3
Author: Iain R. Learmonth <irl@debian.org>
Last-Update: 2016-09-23

---
--- a/tests/applicationinsights_tests/TestTelemetryClient.py
+++ b/tests/applicationinsights_tests/TestTelemetryClient.py
@@ -1,10 +1,15 @@
+import sys
+import os
+import os.path
 import unittest
 import inspect
 import json

-from test import test_support
+if sys.version_info < (3, 0):
+    from test import test_support
+else:
+    from test import support as test_support

-import sys, os, os.path
 rootDirectory = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(__file__)), '..', '..')
 if rootDirectory not in sys.path:
     sys.path.append(rootDirectory)
```

issue_title: WinError on PumpEvents timeout
issue_body:
The CoWaitForMultipleHandles call in the PumpEvents function throws a WindowsError on timeout. Currently, the except statement checks details.args[0] against RPC_S_CALLPENDING (previously defined as -2147417835, which is the winerror number). Details.args[0] contains the "errno". The number we're looking for is in details.args[3], but I think it might be better to refer to details.winerror instead. 

I think it should read: 
```
    try:
        try:
            res = ctypes.oledll.ole32.CoWaitForMultipleHandles(0,
                                                               int(timeout * 1000),
                                                               len(handles), handles,
                                                               ctypes.byref(ctypes.c_ulong()))
        except WindowsError as details:
            if details.winerror != RPC_S_CALLPENDING: # timeout expired
                raise
        else:
            raise KeyboardInterrupt
    finally:
        ctypes.windll.kernel32.CloseHandle(hevt)
        ctypes.windll.kernel32.SetConsoleCtrlHandler(HandlerRoutine, 0)
```

I'm pretty new to all this, but that is how I got it to work in my code.

I'm seeing two sentry messages sent for each exception:

- one as a result of `got_request_exception` signal sent from [here](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/flask/blob/1d49343bb19933f5f8057a548cb69657d8cbd699/flask/app.py#L1537)

- the other as a result of error being logged from [here](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/flask/blob/1d49343bb19933f5f8057a548cb69657d8cbd699/flask/app.py#L1550)

I wouldn't like to set `register_signal=False`, because I'd lose `X-Sentry-Id` header. I wouldn't like to set `logging=False`, because I'd lose capturing of non-exception errors. For now I'm working this around by setting `app.config['RAVEN_IGNORE_EXCEPTIONS'] = [Exception]`.

Is this the expected behaviour? If it is, is there a better way to avoid dupes?

issue_title: correct autogenerated classes
issue_body:
In the interest of providing consistency, I think we could refactor all of the auto generated classes, i.e:

BEFORE:

```

class Profiles_s(Collection):
    """A Collection concrete subclass docstring."""
    def __init__(self, virtual):
        """Auto generated constructor."""
        super(Profiles_s, self).__init__(virtual)
        self._meta_data['allowed_lazy_attributes'] = [Profiles]
        self._meta_data['attribute_registry'] =\
            {u'tm:ltm:virtual:profiles:profilesstate': Profiles}
        self._meta_data['template_generated'] = True
```

AFTER:

```
class Profiles_s(Collection):
    """BIG-IP® LTM profiles subclass collection."""
    def __init__(self, virtual):
        super(Profiles_s, self).__init__(virtual)
        self._meta_data['allowed_lazy_attributes'] = [Profiles]

            {'tm:ltm:virtual:profiles:profilesstate': Profiles}
```

issue_title: Proper handling of updates
issue_body:
**Update**: The ahead mentioned branch was merged. More discussion to enhance updates starts [here](https://github.com/LonamiWebs/Telethon/issues/237#issuecomment-355764566).

---

If anyone is interested in collaborating, please refer to the [better-updates](https://github.com/LonamiWebs/Telethon/tree/better-updates) branch. This is an open source project and personally updates don't bother me that much. If you really need updates, feel free to contribute to Telethon instead reinventing the wheel. I will happily merge.

Currently updates are not handled in their best way, duplicates may be received, we never call [`GetDifferenceRequest`](https://lonamiwebs.github.io/Telethon/methods/updates/get_difference.html), and the managing threads or multiple connections should in general be reworked to properly accommodate this feature.

issue_title: Connection.transport_closed should set self._closed
issue_body:
1. Establish connection `conn = Connection()`
2. Create a channel `ch = conn.channel()`
3. Bring down RabbitMQ broker
4. Attempt an operation on the channel, and SocketTransport detects lost connection and calls `Connection.transport_closed(...)`
5. `conn.closed` still reports `False`

To fix, `Connection.transport_closed()` should also set `self._closed = True`

issue_title: encrypt KeyRing in cryptobox AttributeError
issue_body:
file cryptobox.py line 204 in the encrypt function:

line 204:
`payload_encr = box.encrypt(payload_ser, nonce, encoder=Base64Encoder)`

the box.encrypt() function returns an EncryptedMessage wich is a byte type, but a byte doesnt have an encrypt function

line 205:
`payload_bytes = payload_encr.encode().decode('ascii')`

this line raise "builtins.AttributeError: 'EncryptedMessage' object has no attribute 'encode'"

@BenjaminRepingon managed to fix this issues (with  #735 )by overriding the encrypt function.
with PyNaCl version is at 1.0.1 and authoban version set at 0.16.0

```
class CustomKeyRing(KeyRing):
    def encrypt(self, is_originator, uri, args=None, kwargs=None):
        """
        Encrypt the given WAMP URI, args and kwargs into an EncryptedPayload instance, or None
        if the URI should not be encrypted.
        """
        assert(type(uri) == six.text_type)
        assert(type(is_originator) == bool)
        assert(args is None or type(args) in (list, tuple))
        assert(kwargs is None or type(kwargs) == dict)

        box = self._get_box(is_originator, uri)

        if not box:
            return

        payload = {
            u'uri': uri,
            u'args': args,
            u'kwargs': kwargs
        }
        nonce = random(Box.NONCE_SIZE)

        # dumps return `str` and `box.encrypt` need `bytes`
        payload_ser = json.dumps(payload).encode()

        # box.encrypt return `bytes` (`EncryptedMessage`)
        payload_encr = box.encrypt(payload_ser, nonce, encoder=Base64Encoder)

        if isinstance(payload_encr, bytes):
            # decode directly work, if no decode('ascii') cause an AssertionError
            payload_bytes = payload_encr.decode('ascii')
        else:
            # and this raise "builtins.AttributeError: 'EncryptedMessage' object has no attribute 'encode'"
            payload_bytes = payload_encr.encode().decode('ascii')
        payload_key = None

        return EncryptedPayload(u'cryptobox', payload_key, u'json', payload_bytes)
```

issue_title: Traffic model bug? "optimistic" slower than "best-guess"
issue_body:
Hello, I found that traffic_model="optimistic" sometimes takes longer than traffic_model="best guess". I don't know if this is because of the library or because of Google Maps, but I thought I'd ask, because it makes me question the results using "optimistic" more generally. See attached example, and example Python code:

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6221182/11506788/1c8f5420-981f-11e5-826d-71fc1bceec64.png)

Code output:
['Travel time from: 12.979572,80.25271 to 12.921726,80.230478']
['Departure time (local): 16 Dec 2015 03:42:02']
['Time, no traffic, minutes: 12.566666666666666']
['Time in traffic, minutes: 9.45']
['Time in traffic (optimistic), minutes: 10.45']

Code:
__author__ = 'Gabriel Kreindler, gek@mit.edu'

'''
This code shows that the traffic_model option sometimes generates counter-intuitive results.
The option traffic_model="optimistic" sometimes generates longer travel times compared to "best_guess" or "pessimistic".
(The latter is not shown here.)
'''

import googlemaps
import time
import calendar

# client
client = googlemaps.Client(key='KEY HERE')

# origin and destination
orig="12.979572,80.25271"
dest="12.921726,80.230478"

# query time
t1 = time.strptime("15 Dec 2015 22:12:02", "%d %b %Y %H:%M:%S")
nsec1 = calendar.timegm(t1)

# get offset
timezone_offset = client.timezone(orig, timestamp=nsec1)
timezone_offset_sec = timezone_offset['rawOffset']

# nice local time
t1_loc = time.gmtime(nsec1 + timezone_offset_sec) # local time
dep_time_local = time.strftime("%d %b %Y %H:%M:%S", t1_loc)


# get duration in traffic
dist = client.distance_matrix(orig, dest, departure_time=nsec1, mode="driving")

# parse
temp = dist["rows"][0]["elements"][0]
assert temp["status"]=="OK"
dist_output = temp["distance"]["value"]
dur_output = temp["duration"]["value"]  # duration
dur_intraffic_output = temp["duration_in_traffic"]["value"]

# get duration in traffic
dist = client.distance_matrix(orig, dest, departure_time=nsec1, mode="driving", traffic_model="optimistic")

# parse
temp = dist["rows"][0]["elements"][0]
assert temp["status"]=="OK"
dur_intraffic_opt_output = temp["duration_in_traffic"]["value"]

print(['Travel time from: ' + orig + ' to ' + dest])
print(['Departure time (local): ' + dep_time_local])
print(['Time, no traffic, minutes: ' + str(dur_output/60)])
print(['Time in traffic, minutes: ' + str(dur_intraffic_output/60)])
print(['Time in traffic (optimistic), minutes: ' + str(dur_intraffic_opt_output/60)])

issue_title: 1.4 pollutes output

When running on matplotlib 1.4 with the `classic.mplstyle` file, the output (either when a test fails or when running `pytest -s`) is filled with messages like:

issue_title: Consider removing otPlatRadioIdle() and associated logic.
issue_body: Currently OT uses otPlatRadioIdle() to transition the radio between receive and transmit.  It might be more natural and straightforward if OT were to instead remove the option for a radio Idle State and instead treat the radio as operating in one of the following States {Disabled, Sleep, Receive, Transmit}.  To transmit a packet OT would then make a call to otPlatRadioTransmit() without a pre-requisite call to otPlatRadioIdle().  This also creates an assumption that if the radio is not Disabled, Transmitting or Sleeping then it must be Receiving.  This should reduce opportunities for missing received packets and simplify the OT code.

issue_body:
Hello friends,

I have a weird issue that I'm trying to track down. I'm fiddling with uwsgi and pex, and thus the bootstrapping functions and I've run into something I can't quite figure out.

Hoping @kwlzn can help out, since I borrowed a lot from his pyuwsgi stuff. 

Here's the gist: I've got a flask app that I'm decorating with some attributes (mostly for configs). When I enter the pex interpreter alone, I can access all of the attributes I set, but when I _bootstrap_ the pex environment I am missing the attributes that I add to the `app` object.

issue_title: Standard Error Period Conversion in tears.create_returns_tear_sheet
issue_body:
In **tears.create_returns_tear_sheet**: it appears the following line of code attempts to convert the holding period standard error to a one-period standard error. 

```python
compstd_quant_daily = std_quant_daily.apply(utils.rate_of_return, axis=0)
```

However, while correct for return period conversions, I believe this conversion method is mathematically incorrect for standard deviation/standard error. The correct approximation is below:

se_h = holding period standard error
se_d = one-period standard error
period = holding period length

se_d = se_h / sqrt(period)

```python
import numpy as np
period_len = std_quant_daily.name
compstd_quant_daily = std_quant_daily.apply(lambda x, n: x / np.sqrt(n), args=(period_len,))
```

To get an exact calculation, one would need the expected value and standard deviation of one-period returns. This would be a bit lengthier to fix. For a discussion on the exact calculation, see https://corporate.morningstar.com/US/documents/MethodologyDocuments/MethodologyPapers/SquareRootofTwelve.pdf

issue_title: Add descriptions atop PR curves.
issue_body:
We should pass the summary description stored within SummaryMetadata into the `description` field of `tf-card-heading` within the PR curves dashboard. This is in line with other dashboards and lets the user view rich-text descriptions of each PR curve.

This task will require some reconciling because each PR curve is associated with a tag, and each PR curve can have multiple runs. In turn, each run-tag combination has a description.

The scalars dashboard has logic to address this problem that we should make available to other dashboards.
https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/ba2ef0a2dac5881f37f81a55f17e18b7f9008416/tensorboard/plugins/scalar/tf_scalar_dashboard/tf-scalar-dashboard.html#L295

issue_title: Increase speed of page
issue_body:
Hi! I was looking at the page download speed between native TB icons and the font-awesome. Although font-awesome is far better looking, there's a slight "issue" with downloading too many sources.

For example TB has one file with the icons, and then uses sprites. So, there are 2 files only to download, the css and the icons. Font-awesome does require all font files to be downloaded, which results in separate requests to the server, which consequently takes about 1s for page elements to display in the browser (in my machine when viewing with fresh cache the github page). Could there be a minified embedded base64 encoded version for the css, so fewer files would have to be downloaded?

Thanks!

issue_title: Icon request: icon-cookie
issue_body:
Please consider adding an icon that's the representation of a cookie. 

I am developing a snack site that needs a snack-related icon to represent a section of the site that's a collection of snacks the user has eaten before. The closest thing I could find right now in the font-awesome 4.4 library is a birthday cake, which isn't nearly as great at conveying the concept of "snack" as a cookie would. 

In addition, the cookie icon could also conceivably be used to represent website cookies as another use case. 

The cookie should probably have some kind of a crumbling look to it, since a simple circle or square won't look like a cookie. Maybe it should also have some tiny holes in it like saltines so it looks more like a cookie. 

I've attached an image of something pretty close to what I think would be appropriate. 

Many thanks for your consideration!!

issue_title: [rllib] RunningStat.push error
issue_body:
<!--
General questions should be asked on the mailing list ray-dev@googlegroups.com.

Before submitting an issue, please fill out the following form.
-->

### System information
- **OS Platform and Distribution (e.g., Linux Ubuntu 16.04)**: Linux Ubuntu 16.04
- **Ray installed from (source or binary)**: binary
- **Ray version**: 0.4.0
- **Python version**: 3.5.4
- **Exact command to reproduce**:

### Describe the problem
TypeError: 'numpy.float64' object does not support item assignment  
The error is encounted at line 43 ```self._M[...] += delta / self._n``` In rllib/utils/filter.py.
I think the reason is as follows:  
If the shape of the RunningStat is (), then the type of M in RunningStat is supposed to be numpy.ndarray. But line 46 ```M = (n1 * self._M + n2 * other._M) / n``` makes the type of M become numpy.float64. And numpy.float64 does not support item assignment. So I think line 43 should be ```self._M[...] += delta / self._n```.


### Source code / logs
line 43 in rllib/utils/filter.py.
----------------------- this code works fine -----------------
self._M += delta / self._n
----------------------- this code fails -----------------------
self._M[...] += delta / self._n

issue_title: For date parsing, the time component is being cached between calls.
issue_body:
Maybe this is a "feature" but it smells more like a bug. Reporting in case, since its caused some headaches. 
Using dateparser 0.3.1

Expected behavior:
the "time" part of the datetime object should be the current time when parsing a value with no time info like `today`. 

Current behavior: 
When parsing `today`, if you call it again at a later time the time is being cached in between. Even if calling with a different value like `hoy` (today in spanish).
This is a bit surprising, and I haven't tested what would happen the time rolls over to a different date but I assume that could be problematic also.

```
Python 2.7.10 (default, Sep 30 2015, 17:12:08)
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 7.0.0 (clang-700.0.72)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import dateparser, time
>>> dateparser.parse('today')
datetime.datetime(2015, 11, 11, 17, 28, 50, 234704)
>>> time.sleep(2)
>>> dateparser.parse('today')
datetime.datetime(2015, 11, 11, 17, 28, 50, 234704)
>>> time.sleep(2)
>>> dateparser.parse('hoy')
datetime.datetime(2015, 11, 11, 17, 28, 50, 234704)
```

Thanks! :beers:

issue_title: randSample selection in convConn and divConn not ideal for small networks
issue_body:
The [selection of a random sample](https://github.com/Neurosim-lab/netpyne/blob/development/netpyne/network.py#L834) from the pre/post population using:

    randSample = list(sim.unique([int(round(self.rand.uniform(0,len(preCellsTags)-1))) for i in range(4*convergence)])) # generate 4x and find unique list (to avoid duplicates)

can lead to errors for a small network/convergence size, as for e.g a population of 3 and a conv of 2 will not necessarily yield a list long enough for randSample[convergence] here

     preCellsSample[:] = [randSample[convergence] if x==postCellGid else x for x in preCellsSample] # remove post gid 

to exist. Also, there's no check that randSample[convergence] != postCellGid.

Same for divConn. There's also a finite chance for it happening for larger networks too.

issue_body:
Hello!

Would it be possible to extend the current support for encryption options to makedir() in the S3FS class?

I was excited to be able to use upload_args to pass server side encryption options for file uploads to an s3 bucket. It works fine for file uploads. However, I am unable to create a directory in an s3 bucket that has an encryption policy. Thanks for the consideration!

issue_title: `Measurement` class silently drops attributes on construction and copy
issue_body:
`datacube.model.Measurement` behaves as dictionary/SimpleNamespace, but has surprising behaviours during construction and copy.

- On construction absence of required parameters raises exception
  - Required fields: `name`, `dtype`, `nodata`, `units`
  - Optional fields: `aliases`, `{spectral|flags}_definition`
- Any other key is discarded, silently.

You can however add new fields after construction using `m['somename'] = value`, notation. But if you call `m.copy()` those new items won't be present in the copy. This is surprising behaviour and already caused error in ingestor.

On one hand `Measurement` is a model for measurement definition inside product spec (which only allows 7 keys listed above). On the other hand it is also used as a runtime configuration parameter by data loading pipeline, which injects an extra `resampling_method` parameter (which by the way will end up in the datarray attribute dict, which is questionable).

I'm ok with constructor checking presence of compulsory fields, but ignoring all else, especially silently, is no good. If it is "dict-like with .x access" it should accept any parameter on construction, and certainly not discard things on `copy`.

issue_title: Jaeger client can't parse remote sampling strategy with Python 3.5
issue_body:
## Requirement - what kind of business use case are you trying to solve?

Run Jaeger Python Client with Python 3.5

## Problem - what in Jaeger blocks you from solving the requirement?

Python 3.5 complains about not being able to load JSON with sampling strategy from agent since it's not str but bytes.

```
Fail to parse sampling strategy from jaeger-agent: the JSON object must be str, not 'bytes' [b'{"strategyType":"PROBABILISTIC","probabilisticSampling":{"samplingRate":1}}']
```

## Proposal - what do you suggest to solve the problem or improve the existing situation?

In sampler.py decode `response.body` to str:
```diff
-            sampling_strategies_response = json.loads(response.body)
+            sampling_strategies_response = json.loads(response.body.decode('utf-8'))
```
https://github.com/jaegertracing/jaeger-client-python/blob/master/jaeger_client/sampler.py#L417

## Any open questions to address

None

issue_title: PID coeff in conversion for xl-320
issue_body:
Maybe there is an explanation, but for me the coeff. for the PID gain is a little bit weird : 

```
def dxl_to_pid(value, model):
    return (value[0] * 0.004,
            value[1] * 0.48828125,
            value[2] * 0.125)


def pid_to_dxl(value, model):
    truncate = lambda x: int(max(0, min(x, 254)))
    return [truncate(x * y) for x, y in zip(value, (250, 2.048, 8.0))]
```

Because PID are all beetween 0 and 254 means that when you set in pypot range of : 
P (value[2])  is between 0 and  31,75
I (value[1]) is between 0 and 124,0234375
D (value[0]) is between 0 and 1,016

A good PID in pypot could be (8.0, 15.625, 0.064), I find this not really "readable"

issue_title: Set Window Position documentation incorrect
issue_body:
The entry for the **Set Window Position** documentation is incorrect. It looks like it is copypasta from **Set Window Size**. [Here is the reference](http://robotframework.org/Selenium2Library/doc/Selenium2Library.html#Set%20Window%20Position).

Currently:
```
Set Window Position  x, y  Sets the position x and y of the current window to the specified values.
                           Example:
                           | Set Window Size | ${1000} | ${0}                |
                           | ${x}            | ${y}=   | Get Window Position |
                           | Should Be Equal | ${x}    | ${1000}             |
                           | Should Be Equal | ${y}    | ${0}                |
```
Should be:
```
Set Window Position  x, y  Sets the position x and y of the current window (relative to the left and top of the screen) to the specified values.
                           Example:
                           | Set Window Position | ${8}    | ${10}               |
                           | ${x}                | ${y}=   | Get Window Position |
                           | Should Be Equal     | ${x}    | ${8}                |
                           | Should Be Equal     | ${y}    | ${10}               |
```

issue_title: In prediction, avoid using broadcast, use ModelBroadcast
issue_body:
In prediction, users may directly broadcast model to use it in each task without clone. This is bad because model has its own buffer which cannot be shared among threads.  A better practice is that each task should use a model sharing the parameters but has its own buffer. BigDL provides a ModelBroadcast API for it.

We need to throw some warning if users fall into the first case. Current message is not helpful

issue_title: types: support for LocalProxy on PostgreSQL
issue_body:
SQLAlchemy + PostgreSQL does not handle Werkzeug's LocalProxy when used in queries.
E.g. [this PR](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-deposit/pull/82) add a call to ``str`` around the ``key`` in ``record.files[str(key)`` in order to ensure the a string is passed down instead of a LocalProxy. This should however not be necessary and was already fixed in Invenio 2.0 - most likely using the feature:
http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/core/compiler.html#changing-compilation-of-types

issue_title: widget not responsive in HTML
Precompute possible outcomes of interact and include them in static HTML export
issue_body:
I suggest adding a feature to the Ipywidgets, that allows to precompute all possible outcomes of the function. These precomputed results should then be displayed when the user moves the slider of an interact statement. This should work even if the Jupyter notebook is exported to HTML.
Let's take the following sample code:
```python
from ipywidgets import interact
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

def f(a):
    x = np.linspace(0,2,100)
    plt.plot(x,a*x**2)
    plt.ylim([0,20])
    plt.show()
    return None

interact(f, a=(1,5,1))
```
In the final HTML file, all possible images (in this case all parabolas with a=1...5) should be included (in this sense pre-computed) and then displayed when the user selects the appropriate value with the slider. Of course, this selection has to be done by JavaScript since for a "stand alone" HTML file no Python kernel is available. I'm aware that this can increase the file size tremendously depending on the number of possible outcomes. Nevertheless, the precomputation implemented in a handy way would improve the ipython-widgets project in my opinion.

Peter

issue_title: Allow compatible objects in `Select` and `SelectMultiple` to render as HTML.
issue_body:
The attribute `choice` for `Select` and `SelectMultiple` objects accepts arbitrary Python objects, and will use  the objects' `__str__` method to render each entry in the selection list.

It would be adding a lot of visual expressivily  if objects in the selection list that are `HTML`, `HTMLMath`, `Image` could be rendered as HTML rather than strings.  Optionally, `Box`, `HBox` and `Vbox` could also be allowed to render as HTML their components whenever possible.

issue_title: reads with samflags 99/147 have reference coordinates differing by one compared to 88/163
issue_body:
Hi all,

I am using pysam to parse paired-end whole-genome bisulfute sequencing bam files generated by Bismark/Bowtie2. 

I am using pysam to pull out all reads in specific regions. For reads with samflags 88 or 163 everything appears to be as expected. For example: If i use `read.get_reference_positions()` and compare the bases at these positions back reference back to the genome I find the base I expect, in my case  the CpG I am looking for. 

However if the read has samflags of 99 or 147 the base reference position appears to be shifted by one compard to its expected reference position in the genome. For example, when the reference position should point to a CpG it actually points to an A  in a ACG or C in a CCG or even a G in a GCG. No matter the context they appears to be off by one base-pair.

Is this a bug? Or is there some underlying function I am just misunderstanding that has something to do with these samflags?

Thanks for your time.

issue_title: Bridge.get_light_objects() can return stale data
issue_body:
If new lights are added to the bridge (and presumably also if existing ones are removed) after the Bridge object is instantiated, the return value of get_light_objects() does not reflect the changes. Looking at the code, it appears that get_light_objects just caches the data after first requesting it from the bridge, then never updates it again.

At the very least, there should be a way to explicitly tell the Bridge object to do a refetch of the data. But I would propose simply having get_light_objects fetch the data anew from the bridge each time. Unless there's some other way to know if the lights have actually changed, this seems to be the only sensible thing to do. If performance of polling the bridge is a concern, it's simple enough for users of the module to save the return value themselves if necessary.

issue_title: Feature: Configure maximum session length
issue_body:
Currently, some backends can provide an expiration length on the identity that they provide. However, others do not, and by default, the sessions produced by these providers will be equal to the Django platform default (2 weeks, if no other settings are made).

In some cases, it may be desirable to restrict SSO login users to a shorter session length, or even set their sessions to expire on browser close. The pull request accompanying this issue adds a setting that can be checked to determine the maximum length of session that should be set up for the incoming user (setting this variable to 0 will set a session cookie that will be discarded on browser close).

issue_title: [Docs] Example of polling a bigquery job is wrong.
issue_body:
`job.state == 'running'` does not exhaustively describe the non-complete states (and in fact is not a valid state at all as running jobs have state `RUNNING`

But since jobs can also have state `PENDING` it should be `not job.state == 'DONE'`

issue_title: Fetching from remote with explicit refspec raises error incorrectly
issue_body:
GitPython version: 1.0.2
File: git/remote.py
Line: 646
OS: Ubuntu 14.04LTS

Passing a refspec to git fetch through a remote, as in
`repo.remotes.origin.fetch("+refs/heads/*:refs/remotes/origin/*")`
will raise an error if the remote has no fetch refspec defined in the config:
```
AssertionError: Remote '%s' has no refspec set.
You can set it as follows: 'git config --add "remote.origin.fetch +refs/heads/*:refs/heads/*"'.
```
Also, note that the error message is incorrectly formatted because the remote's name is replaced only in the second line of the message (a %s appears in the first line of the final message).

The `_assert_refspec()` method should not be called if one or more refspecs were given as arguments to `fetch()`.

issue_title: non-scatter fields in scatter workflow steps not working
issue_body: When using _StepInputExpressionRequirement_ step inputs with valueFrom should be mapped according to the [WorkflowStepInput specification](http://www.commonwl.org/draft-3/Workflow.html#WorkflowStepInput). There is a bug when using scatter steps, where the valueFrom field is not checked for non-scatter inputs (resulting in a _None_ value).
